content:
# Luke Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 109-110 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_017_page_001.jpg, LUK_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Jesus said to his disciples, "It is inevitable that things will come that cause people to stumble into sin, but how terrible it will be for the person through whom they come! **2** It would be better for that person to have a heavy millstone hung around his neck and be thrown into the sea than to cause one of these vulnerable ones to stumble. **3** Watch yourselves carefully. If your brother sins, confront him, and if he repents, forgive him. **4** Even if he sins against you seven times in a single day and comes back to you seven times saying, 'I repent,' you must forgive him."

**5** The apostles said to the Lord, "Give us more faith!" **6** The Lord replied, "If you had faith even as small as a mustard seed, you could say to this mulberry tree, 'Be uprooted and planted in the sea,' and it would obey you.

**7** "Suppose one of you has a servant who has been plowing fields or tending sheep. When the servant comes in from the field, would you say to him, 'Come right in and sit down to eat'? **8** Of course not. Instead, you would say, 'Prepare my dinner, put on your apron, and serve me while I eat and drink. After that, you may eat and drink.' **9** Does the master owe the servant special thanks because he did what he was told? **10** It is the same with you: when you have done everything you were instructed to do, say, 'We are unworthy servants; we have only done what was our duty.'"

**11** Now as Jesus was on his way to Jerusalem, he was traveling along the border between Samaria and Galilee. **12** As he entered a certain village, ten men suffering from leprosy met him. They stood at a distance **13** and called out in loud voices, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!" **14** When he saw them, he said, "Go and show yourselves to the priests." And as they were on their way, they were healed.

**15** One of them, when he realized he had been healed, turned back, praising God at the top of his voice. **16** He threw himself facedown at Jesus' feet, thanking him — and he was a Samaritan. **17** Jesus responded, "Were not all ten healed? Where are the other nine? **18** Was no one found to come back and give praise to God except this foreigner?" **19** Then he said to the man, "Get up and go on your way. Your faith has made you well."

**20** When the Pharisees asked Jesus when the kingdom of God would come, he answered them, "The kingdom of God does not come in ways you can observe with your eyes. **21** People will not say, 'Look, here it is!' or 'There it is!' For the kingdom of God is already among you."

**22** Then he said to his disciples, "The time is coming when you will long to see even one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it. **23** People will say to you, 'Look, there he is!' or 'Look, here he is!' Do not go running after them. **24** For just as lightning flashes and lights up the entire sky from one horizon to the other, so will the Son of Man be on his day. **25** But first he must suffer terribly and be rejected by this generation.

**26** "And just as it was in the days of Noah, so it will be in the days of the Son of Man. **27** People were eating, drinking, marrying, and being given in marriage right up to the day Noah entered the ark — and then the flood came and destroyed them all. **28** It was the same in the days of Lot: people were eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, and building. **29** But on the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven and destroyed them all. **30** It will be just like that on the day the Son of Man is revealed.

**31** "On that day, anyone who is on the roof of his house must not go down to get his belongings from inside. Likewise, anyone who is in the field must not turn back for anything. **32** Remember Lot's wife! **33** Whoever tries to preserve his life will lose it, and whoever loses his life will keep it alive.

**34** "I tell you, on that night two people will be in one bed — one will be taken and the other left. **35** Two women will be grinding grain together — one will be taken and the other left." **36** [This verse is not present in the Sinaiticus manuscript.]

**37** His disciples asked him, "Where, Lord?" He answered them, "Wherever there is a carcass, there the vultures will gather."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **17:1-2 — "things that cause people to stumble" and "a heavy millstone"**: The word SKANDALA (skandala, "stumbling blocks" or "snares") originally referred to the trigger of a trap — the stick that, when touched, springs the mechanism shut. Jesus declares that such enticements to sin are ANENDEKTON (anendekton, "inevitable" or "unavoidable") — they are a given in this fallen world. But inevitability does not equal innocence. The person through whom the stumbling block comes faces a OUAI (ouai, "woe") — not merely sorrow but a prophetic declaration of doom. The image of a LITHOS MYLIKOS (lithos mylikos, "millstone") hung PERI TON TRACHĒLON (peri ton trachēlon, "around the neck") and a drowning in the sea communicates that there is something worse than physical death: causing a MIKRŌN TOUTŌN (mikrōn toutōn, "one of these little ones" — likely referring to new or vulnerable believers) to fall away from faith. The millstone Jesus specifies is the heavy upper stone turned by a donkey, not the small hand-mill stone used by women — this is industrial-scale imagery for an industrial-scale crime.

- **17:3-4 — Confrontation, repentance, and forgiveness**: The command structure here is precise: EPITIMĒSON (epitimēson, "rebuke him" or "confront him") comes first — silence in the face of a brother's sin is not love but complicity. But if he METANOĒSĒ (metanoēsē, "repents"), then APHES AUTŌ (aphes autō, "release him" or "forgive him"). The radical escalation in verse 4 — seven offenses in a single day, each followed by the words METANOŌ (metanoō, "I repent") — tests whether forgiveness is a genuine commitment or merely a polite gesture. Seven is not a mathematical limit but a number signifying completeness; Jesus is saying that forgiveness must have no ceiling. The disciples' response in verse 5 ("Give us more faith!") shows they understand the demand correctly — this kind of forgiveness is beyond natural human capacity.

- **17:5-6 — Faith as small as a mustard seed**: The apostles' request — PROSTHES HĒMIN PISTIN (prosthes hēmin pistin, "add faith to us" or "increase our faith") — assumes that faith is a quantity that needs to be enlarged. Jesus' reply subverts the assumption entirely. He does not say, "Very well, here is more faith." He says, "If you had faith like a KOKKON SINAPEŌS (kokkon sinapeōs, 'grain of mustard') ..." — the smallest seed known in Palestinian agriculture. The point is that faith is not measured by size but by authenticity. Even the tiniest genuine trust in God can do what seems impossible — here represented by commanding a SYKAMINŌ (sykaminō, "mulberry tree" or "sycamine tree"), a deeply rooted tree, to uproot itself and plant itself in the sea. The absurdity of the image is the message: real faith operates in a different category entirely from human calculation. The problem is never too little faith but no faith — the difference between a mustard seed and an empty hand.

- **17:7-10 — The parable of the unworthy servant**: This parable, unique to Luke, is a corrective to any sense of spiritual entitlement that might arise from the preceding teaching. If faith can move trees, the disciples might begin to feel powerful. Jesus immediately redirects: a servant (DOULOS, doulos — literally "slave") who has spent the day AROTRIŌNTA (arotriōnta, "plowing") or POIMAINONONTA (poimainononta, "shepherding") does not come home to be waited on. He comes home to serve dinner. And when he has done everything commanded, he earns no special CHARIN (charin, "thanks" or "gratitude") — he has simply done TO OPHEILOMENON (to opheilomenon, "what was owed" or "what was due"). The application in verse 10 is startling: DOULOI ACHREIOI ESMEN (douloi achreioi esmen, "we are useless servants" or "we are unworthy servants"). The word ACHREIOI does not mean they have done badly — they have done everything commanded. It means they have no grounds for claiming extra merit. The parable guards against the transactional distortion of faith: obedience is not a currency that purchases divine favor. It is simply what servants do.

- **17:11 — "traveling along the border between Samaria and Galilee"**: The phrase DIA MESON SAMAREIAS KAI GALILAIAS (dia meson Samareias kai Galilaias, "through the middle of Samaria and Galilee") describes Jesus' route toward Jerusalem along the boundary zone between the two regions — a liminal space where Jewish and Samaritan communities lived in uneasy proximity. This geographical detail is not incidental: it sets the stage for the story that follows, in which a Samaritan will prove to be the only grateful leper. Luke has been building the Samaritan theme throughout his Gospel (9:51-56, the rejected village; 10:25-37, the Good Samaritan), and this border setting physically embodies the social boundary that Jesus' ministry repeatedly crosses.

- **17:12-14 — The ten lepers**: The lepers stand PORRŌTHEN (porrōthen, "at a distance") because Levitical law required it (Leviticus 13:45-46) — they are ritually unclean and must not approach. Their cry — EPISTATA (epistata, "Master" or "Commander," a title used exclusively in Luke's Gospel) — is both a plea and an act of faith. Jesus' instruction to show themselves to the priests follows the procedure prescribed in Leviticus 14 for the certification of healing. But the remarkable detail is that the healing happens EN TŌ HYPAGEIN AUTOUS (en tō hypagein autous, "as they were going") — not before they obeyed but during their obedience. They had to walk toward the priests while still leprous, trusting that healing would come. This is faith in motion: acting on Jesus' word before the evidence arrives.

- **17:15-18 — The grateful Samaritan**: Only one of the ten returns, and Luke emphasizes three actions: he turns back (HYPESTREPSEN, hypestrepsen), he glorifies God with a loud voice (META PHŌNĒS MEGALĒS DOXAZŌN TON THEON), and he falls on his face at Jesus' feet (EPESEN EPI PROSŌPON PARA TOUS PODAS AUTOU). The climactic detail — KAI AUTOS ĒN SAMAREITĒS (kai autos ēn Samareitēs, "and he was a Samaritan") — is placed at the end for maximum impact. Jesus' three questions in verses 17-18 are devastating: Were not ten healed? Where are the nine? Did none return except this ALLOGENĒS (allogenēs, "foreigner" or "one of another race")? The word ALLOGENĒS was inscribed on the barrier in the Jerusalem temple beyond which non-Jews could not pass on pain of death. Jesus uses the temple's own exclusionary language to shame the nine Jewish lepers who did not return. The outsider understood what the insiders missed: that healing demands gratitude, and gratitude demands return.

- **17:19 — "Your faith has made you well"**: The phrase HĒ PISTIS SOU SESŌKEN SE (hē pistis sou sesōken se) can mean both "your faith has healed you" and "your faith has saved you." All ten were healed (EKATHARISTHĒSAN, ekatharisthēsan, "were cleansed") in verse 14, but only the Samaritan hears SESŌKEN (sesōken, "has saved"). Luke is drawing a distinction between physical healing, which all ten received, and the deeper wholeness — salvation — that comes through faith expressed in gratitude and worship. The nine received a medical cure; the Samaritan received rescue for his entire being.

- **17:20-21 — "The kingdom of God is among you"**: The Pharisees' question about WHEN (POTE, pote) the kingdom comes reveals their expectation of a visible, datable, political event — the overthrow of Rome and the restoration of Israel's sovereignty. Jesus' answer demolishes this framework. The kingdom does not come META PARATĒRĒSEŌS (meta paratērēseōs, "with careful observation" or "with signs to be observed") — you cannot track it the way you track an advancing army or a celestial phenomenon. The crucial phrase ENTOS HYMŌN ESTIN (entos hymōn estin) has been debated for centuries. ENTOS can mean "within you" (the kingdom as an interior spiritual reality) or "among you" / "in your midst" (the kingdom as present in the person of Jesus himself). Given that Jesus is addressing the Pharisees — hardly candidates for an interior spiritual kingdom — the meaning "among you" or "in your midst" is more likely. The kingdom is not a future event to be calculated; it is a present reality standing before them in the person of the one they are questioning.

- **17:22-25 — Longing for the Son of Man's day**: Jesus shifts from addressing the Pharisees to speaking privately with his disciples, and the tone changes from present-kingdom theology to future-expectation warning. The disciples will EPITHYMĒSETE (epithymēsete, "long for" or "desire intensely") to see MIAN TŌN HĒMERŌN TOU HUIOU TOU ANTHRŌPOU (mian tōn hēmerōn tou huiou tou anthrōpou, "one of the days of the Son of Man") — even a single day of his visible presence — and will not see it. This suggests a period of absence, suffering, and longing for the early church. During this period, false reports will circulate: "Look here!" "Look there!" The command is absolute: MĒ APELTHĒTE MĒDE DIŌXĒTE (mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte, "do not go away, do not pursue"). The genuine coming will need no announcement — like ASTRAPĒ (astrapē, "lightning") that illuminates the entire sky from horizon to horizon, it will be unmistakable and universal. But verse 25 inserts a necessary prerequisite: DEI AUTON POLLA PATHEIN (dei auton polla pathein, "he must first suffer many things") — the glory cannot come without the cross.

- **17:26-30 — The days of Noah and Lot**: Jesus draws two parallel illustrations from Genesis. In Noah's time (Genesis 6-8) and Lot's time (Genesis 18-19), people were engaged in entirely ordinary activities — ĒSTHION, EPINON, EGAMOUN, EGAMIZONTO (eating, drinking, marrying, being given in marriage) for Noah's generation; ĒSTHION, EPINON, ĒGORAZON, EPOLOUN, EPHYTEUON, ŌKODOMOUN (eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, building) for Lot's. The extended list for Lot's generation adds commercial and agricultural activities — the routines of a prosperous, settled civilization. The point is not that these activities are sinful but that they had become the totality of people's awareness. Life was so thoroughly consumed by the ordinary that the extraordinary judgment came as a complete shock. The Son of Man's day will arrive with the same suddenness — KATAKLUMOS (kataklusmos, "the flood") for Noah's generation, PYR KAI THEION (pyr kai theion, "fire and sulfur") for Lot's. The warning is against the comfortable assumption that tomorrow will be like today.

- **17:31-33 — "Remember Lot's wife"**: This is the shortest sentence Jesus speaks in Luke's Gospel, and one of the most powerful. The command not to turn back — neither from the DŌMA (dōma, "rooftop" or "housetop") to retrieve possessions nor from the AGROS (agros, "field") to collect belongings — echoes the urgency of Lot's flight from Sodom (Genesis 19:17, "Do not look back"). Lot's wife (Genesis 19:26) looked back and became a pillar of salt. She was physically escaping but emotionally attached. The principle in verse 33 crystallizes it: HOS EAN ZĒTĒSĒ TĒN PSYCHĒN AUTOU PERIPOIĒSASTHAI (hos ean zētēsē tēn psychēn autou peripoiēsasthai, "whoever seeks to preserve his life") will APOLESEI (apolesei, "destroy" or "lose") it, and whoever APOLESĒ (apolesē, "loses" it) will ZŌOGONĒSEI (zōogonēsei, "make it alive" or "preserve it alive"). The verb ZŌOGONĒSEI is striking — it means "to bring to life" or "to give birth to life," suggesting that losing one's grip on self-preservation is not death but a second birth.

- **17:34-35 — Taken and left**: The imagery shifts to nighttime (two in one bed) and daytime (two women grinding at a mill), suggesting that the Son of Man's coming will affect everyone regardless of the time. In each pair, one is PARALĒMPHTHĒSETAI (paralēmphthēsetai, "will be taken along" or "received") and the other APHETHĒSETAI (aphethēsetai, "will be left" or "abandoned"). The meaning of "taken" versus "left" has been much debated. In the context of the Noah and Lot analogies, being "taken" could mean being swept away in judgment (as the flood "took" people in Noah's day), or it could mean being received into safety (as Lot was taken out of Sodom). The ambiguity may be deliberate: the point is not to decode the mechanism but to reckon with the reality that the closest human relationships — sharing a bed, working side by side — will not determine one's fate. The division cuts through the most intimate bonds.

- **17:36 — The missing verse**: Codex Sinaiticus does not contain verse 36 ("Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left"), which appears in later manuscripts, likely imported from Matthew 24:40. This is a significant textual variant: the Sinaiticus tradition moves directly from the two women grinding (v. 35) to the disciples' question (v. 37).

- **17:37 — "Wherever there is a carcass, the vultures will gather"**: The disciples ask POU (pou, "where?") — they want a location, a geography of judgment. Jesus answers with a proverb: HOPOU TO SŌMA, EKEI KAI HOI AETOI EPISYNACHTHĒSONTAI (hopou to sōma, ekei kai hoi aetoi episynachthēsontai, "where the body is, there also the eagles/vultures will be gathered"). The word AETOI (aetoi) can mean either "eagles" or "vultures" — both are large carrion birds in Palestinian usage. The SŌMA (sōma) here means "corpse" rather than "living body." The proverb means: judgment does not need to be announced or located on a map. It will find its target as inevitably as vultures find a carcass. The decomposition attracts the scavengers; the corruption attracts the judgment. This is Jesus' final refusal to answer apocalyptic speculation with a timeline or a geography. The question "where?" receives the answer "wherever it is needed." The coming of the Son of Man is not an event to be predicted but a reality to be prepared for.

- **17:1-37 — The chapter as a whole**: Luke 17 weaves together four seemingly disparate units — instruction on sin and forgiveness (vv. 1-4), the nature of faith and service (vv. 5-10), the healing of the ten lepers (vv. 11-19), and the coming of the Son of Man (vv. 20-37) — into a coherent meditation on readiness. Each section asks the same fundamental question in a different register: Are you prepared? Prepared to forgive without limit? Prepared to serve without entitlement? Prepared to recognize God's work and respond with gratitude? Prepared for the sudden, inescapable arrival of the Son of Man? The Samaritan leper stands at the chapter's physical and thematic center — the one person who turned back, who recognized what had happened, who responded appropriately. In a chapter filled with warnings about people who miss the moment (the nine lepers, Noah's generation, Lot's neighbors, Lot's wife), the Samaritan is the image of readiness: he saw, he turned, he worshipped, and he was saved.
